.
Which section contains the following information?

A-G
, in boxes 14–18 on your answer sheet.

obj.title: Return of the elm: reintroducing the beloved tree to Britain
obj.content:
[br/][center]Mark Rowe investigates attempts to reintroduce elms to Britain[/center][br/][br/]
[p:a]Around 25 million elms, accounting for 90% of all elm trees in the UK, died during the 1960s and '70s of Dutch elm disease. In the aftermath, the elm, once so dominant in the British landscape, was largely forgotten. However, there's now hope the elm may be reintroduced to the countryside of central and southern England. Any reintroduction will start from a very low base. 'The impact of the disease is difficult to picture if you hadn't seen what was there before,' says Matt Elliot of the Woodland Trust. 'You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it's only then that you realise the impact [elms had] … They were significant, large trees… then they were gone.'[br/][br/]
[p:b]The disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the elms' vascular (water, nutrient and food transport) system, causing branches to wilt and die. A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the '70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. This time, the beetles carried a much more virulent strain that destroyed the vast majority of British elms.[br][br]
[p:c]Today, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. 'We have millions of small elms in hedgerows but they get targeted by the beetle as soon as they reach a certain size,' says Karen Russell, co-author of the report 'Where we are with elm'. Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10-15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold. Yet mature specimens have been identified, in counties such as Cambridgeshire, that are hundreds of years old, and have mysteriously escaped the epidemic.[br][br]
The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small. 'What are the reasons for their survival?' asks Russell. 'Avoidance, tolerance, resistance? We don't know where the balance lies between the three. I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck.'[br][br]
[p:d]For centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. Elm was also the preferred material for shields and early swords. In the 18th century, it was planted more widely and its wood was used for items such as storage crates and flooring. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.[br][br]
[p:e]Given how ingrained elm is in British culture, it's unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. 'I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,' he says. 'The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.' Today, the city of Brighton's elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city's elm population. However, the situation is precarious. 'The beetles can just march in if we're not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,' says Bourne.[br][br]
[p:f]Any prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit on the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. 'The effects are very quick,' says Russell. 'You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.'[br][br]
[p:g]All of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. 'If we're putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native – are we bothered about that?' For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. 'They will host wildlife, which is a good thing. Others are more wary. 'On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,' says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, 'You're replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You're effectively cloning.' There's also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. 'Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover over time, you might get resistance,' says Elliot.[br][br]
* horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species
obj.content_zh:
[br/][center]马克·罗（Mark Rowe）对英国重新引种榆树的计划进行了调研。[/center][br/][br/]
[b] A. [/b] 20世纪60年代至70年代，英国约2500万棵榆树(占英国所有榆树的90%)死于荷兰榆树病。此后，曾经在英国景观中占主导地位的榆树，很大程度上被遗忘了。然而，现在有希望将榆树重新引入英格兰中部和南部的乡村地区。所有重新引入的工作都将从一个非常低的起点开始。“如果你没有见过那里以前的景象，就很难想象这种疾病带来的影响，“林地信托基金的马特·埃利奥特说。“你得看看20世纪60年代的老照片，才会意识到榆树曾经的影响(有多大)它们(那时)是重要的巨大树木..接着就消失了。[br/][br/]
[b] B. [/b] 这种疾病是由一种真菌引起的，这种真菌会阻塞榆树的维管系统(水分、营养和食物运输系统)，导致树枝枯萎死亡。该病20世纪20年代发生的第一次疫情/传播逐渐平息，但在70年代，从加拿大运来的榆树引发了第二次传播。这些木材是原木形式运来的，用于造船，其完好的树皮为传播这种致命真菌的榆树皮甲虫提供了理想的生存环境。这一次，甲虫携带了一种毒性更强的菌株，摧毁了英国绝大多数的榆树。[br][br]
[b] C. [/b] 如今，英格兰南部的乡村仍有榆树，但大多只存在于田野间低矮的树篱中。“在树篱中有数百万棵小榆树，但它们一长到特定大小就会成为甲虫的目标，“《榆树现状报告》的合著者卡伦·拉塞尔说。“一旦榆树的树干直径达到10-15厘米左右就达到了甲虫产卵和真菌滋生的理想大小。然而，在剑桥郡等郡，人们发现了一些有数百年树龄的成熟榆树，它们不可思议地躲过了这场疫病。[br][br]
拉塞尔说，关键是要找出并研究那些存活下来的树木，弄清楚为什么在数百万棵榆树病死的情况下，它们还能存活下来。然而，由于这些存活到成熟的榆树相对较少，研究机会有限。“它们存活下来的原因是什么?“拉塞尔问道。“是它们避开疫病，还是对这种病有耐受力或者抵抗力?(但是)我们不知道这三者之间的平衡在哪里。我不认为这完全是运气。“[注释:"Where we arewith elm'是一份关于榆树现状的报告。[br][br]
[b] D. [/b] 几个世纪以来，在英国，榆树作为硬木树种在受欢迎程度方面仅次于橡树，而且在许多情况下，它是景观中最显眼的树。榆树不仅在欧洲森林中常见，还是桦树、白蜡树和榛树林的重要组成部分。榆树的使用可以追溯到青铜时代，当时它被广泛用于(制作)工具。榆树也是制盾和早期制剑的首选材料。18世纪，榆树被更广泛地种植，其木材被用于制作储物箱和地板等物品。它也适用于(制作)承受高强度撞击的物品，19世纪的帆船“卡蒂萨克号“的龙骨以及采矿设备就都是用榆树制作的。[br][br]
[b] E. [/b] 鉴于榆树在英国文化中根深蒂固，它有很多拥护者也就不足为奇了，比如布莱顿国家榆树收藏馆的彼得·伯恩。他说:“我小时候目睹了荷兰榆树病的蔓延。那时候榆树似乎是英格兰乡村的一部分，但我记得我眼睁睁地看着树叶凋零，那种景象真的挥之不去。“如今，布莱顿市的榆树总数约17000棵。似乎当地有些因素能够帮助它们存活下来。来自海上的强风使顽固的榆树皮甲虫难以攻击这座沿海城市的榆树群。然而，情况仍然岌岌可危。“威胁近在咫尺，要是我们不小心，甲虫就会入侵。“伯恩说。[br][br]
[b] F. [/b] 榆树未来是否能够回归，很大程度上取决于树木对这种疾病是否具有抵抗力或耐受力。这意味着大规模重新引入将涉及(使用)现有的或新的杂交品种，这些品种源自有抵抗力的、通常是非本地的榆树物种。新一代的幼苗已经培育出来并进行了测试:通过在树皮上切一个小切口并注入少量病原体，来观察它们是否能抵抗这种真菌。“效果很快就会显现，“拉塞尔说。“四到六周后你再去看这些树，有抵抗力的树木没有任何症状，而易受感染的树则会落叶，甚至可能完全死亡。[br][br]
[b] G. [/b] 拉塞尔承认，所有这些(做法)会引发社会接受度的问题。“如果我们把榆树重新引入当地/景观中，且其中有一小部分是非本地的树--我们会担心吗?“对她来说，重新引入榆树，从环境角度出发，理由是很充分的。“它们会为野生动物提供栖息地，这是一件好事。“其他人则更为谨慎。"表面上看，这似乎是个好主意，“埃利奥特说。他认为问题在于，“你在用一种园艺类似物取代本地物种。实际上你是在克隆。“这还会有引入新疾病的风险。林地信托基金强调的是要为那些独立存活下来的榆树提供生长空间，而不是主张种植新的榆树。“有时候，你能做到最好的事情，就是(这些给大自然时间去恢复.…随着时间的推移，榆树)可能会产生抵抗力。“埃利奥特说。[br][br]
*园艺类似物:一种与现有物种基因相似的栽培物物种*[注释:“园艺类似物“就是人工培育的、与天然物种相似的“替代品”。
obj.html_content:
Mark Rowe investigates attempts to reintroduce elms to Britain




aAround 25 million elms, accounting for 90% of all elm trees in the UK, died during the 1960s and '70s of Dutch elm disease. In the aftermath, the elm, once so dominant in the British landscape, was largely forgotten. However, there's now hope the elm may be reintroduced to the countryside of central and southern England. Any reintroduction will start from a very low base. 'The impact of the disease is difficult to picture if you hadn't seen what was there before,' says Matt Elliot of the Woodland Trust. 'You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it's only then that you realise the impact [elms had] … They were significant, large trees… then they were gone.'



bThe disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the elms' vascular (water, nutrient and food transport) system, causing branches to wilt and die. A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the '70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. This time, the beetles carried a much more virulent strain that destroyed the vast majority of British elms.

cToday, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. 'We have millions of small elms in hedgerows but they get targeted by the beetle as soon as they reach a certain size,' says Karen Russell, co-author of the report 'Where we are with elm'. Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10-15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold. Yet mature specimens have been identified, in counties such as Cambridgeshire, that are hundreds of years old, and have mysteriously escaped the epidemic.

The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small. 'What are the reasons for their survival?' asks Russell. 'Avoidance, tolerance, resistance? We don't know where the balance lies between the three. I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck.'

dFor centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. Elm was also the preferred material for shields and early swords. In the 18th century, it was planted more widely and its wood was used for items such as storage crates and flooring. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.

eGiven how ingrained elm is in British culture, it's unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. 'I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,' he says. 'The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.' Today, the city of Brighton's elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city's elm population. However, the situation is precarious. 'The beetles can just march in if we're not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,' says Bourne.

fAny prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit on the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. 'The effects are very quick,' says Russell. 'You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.'

gAll of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. 'If we're putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native – are we bothered about that?' For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. 'They will host wildlife, which is a good thing. Others are more wary. 'On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,' says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, 'You're replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You're effectively cloning.' There's also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. 'Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover over time, you might get resistance,' says Elliot.

* horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species

added_content.ex_information:
Look at the following statements (Questions 19–23) and the list of people below.
Match each statement with the correct person,
A
,
B
, or
C
.
NB
You may use any letter more than once.

obj.content: If a tree gets infected with Dutch elm disease, the damage rapidly becomes visible.[match]19[/match]
obj.html_content: If a tree gets infected with Dutch elm disease, the damage rapidly becomes visible.19

obj.content: It may be better to wait and see if the mature elms that have survived continue to flourish.[match]20[/match]
obj.html_content: It may be better to wait and see if the mature elms that have survived continue to flourish.20

obj.content: There must be an explanation for the survival of some mature elms.[match]21[/match]
obj.html_content: There must be an explanation for the survival of some mature elms.21

obj.content: We need to be aware that insects carrying Dutch elm disease are not very far away.[match]22[/match]
obj.html_content: We need to be aware that insects carrying Dutch elm disease are not very far away.22

obj.content: You understand the effect Dutch elm disease has had when you see evidence of how prominent the tree once was.[match]23[/match]
obj.html_content: You understand the effect Dutch elm disease has had when you see evidence of how prominent the tree once was.23

obj.content_zh:
[br/][center]马克·罗（Mark Rowe）对英国重新引种榆树的计划进行了调研。[/center][br/][br/]
[b] A. [/b] 20世纪60年代至70年代，英国约2500万棵榆树(占英国所有榆树的90%)死于荷兰榆树病。此后，曾经在英国景观中占主导地位的榆树，很大程度上被遗忘了。然而，现在有希望将榆树重新引入英格兰中部和南部的乡村地区。所有重新引入的工作都将从一个非常低的起点开始。“如果你没有见过那里以前的景象，就很难想象这种疾病带来的影响，“林地信托基金的马特·埃利奥特说。“你得看看20世纪60年代的老照片，才会意识到榆树曾经的影响(有多大)它们(那时)是重要的巨大树木..接着就消失了。[br/][br/]
[b] B. [/b] 这种疾病是由一种真菌引起的，这种真菌会阻塞榆树的维管系统(水分、营养和食物运输系统)，导致树枝枯萎死亡。该病20世纪20年代发生的第一次疫情/传播逐渐平息，但在70年代，从加拿大运来的榆树引发了第二次传播。这些木材是原木形式运来的，用于造船，其完好的树皮为传播这种致命真菌的榆树皮甲虫提供了理想的生存环境。这一次，甲虫携带了一种毒性更强的菌株，摧毁了英国绝大多数的榆树。[br][br]
[b] C. [/b] 如今，英格兰南部的乡村仍有榆树，但大多只存在于田野间低矮的树篱中。“在树篱中有数百万棵小榆树，但它们一长到特定大小就会成为甲虫的目标，“《榆树现状报告》的合著者卡伦·拉塞尔说。“一旦榆树的树干直径达到10-15厘米左右就达到了甲虫产卵和真菌滋生的理想大小。然而，在剑桥郡等郡，人们发现了一些有数百年树龄的成熟榆树，它们不可思议地躲过了这场疫病。[br][br]
拉塞尔说，关键是要找出并研究那些存活下来的树木，弄清楚为什么在数百万棵榆树病死的情况下，它们还能存活下来。然而，由于这些存活到成熟的榆树相对较少，研究机会有限。“它们存活下来的原因是什么?“拉塞尔问道。“是它们避开疫病，还是对这种病有耐受力或者抵抗力?(但是)我们不知道这三者之间的平衡在哪里。我不认为这完全是运气。“[注释:"Where we arewith elm'是一份关于榆树现状的报告。[br][br]
[b] D. [/b] 几个世纪以来，在英国，榆树作为硬木树种在受欢迎程度方面仅次于橡树，而且在许多情况下，它是景观中最显眼的树。榆树不仅在欧洲森林中常见，还是桦树、白蜡树和榛树林的重要组成部分。榆树的使用可以追溯到青铜时代，当时它被广泛用于(制作)工具。榆树也是制盾和早期制剑的首选材料。18世纪，榆树被更广泛地种植，其木材被用于制作储物箱和地板等物品。它也适用于(制作)承受高强度撞击的物品，19世纪的帆船“卡蒂萨克号“的龙骨以及采矿设备就都是用榆树制作的。[br][br]
[b] E. [/b] 鉴于榆树在英国文化中根深蒂固，它有很多拥护者也就不足为奇了，比如布莱顿国家榆树收藏馆的彼得·伯恩。他说:“我小时候目睹了荷兰榆树病的蔓延。那时候榆树似乎是英格兰乡村的一部分，但我记得我眼睁睁地看着树叶凋零，那种景象真的挥之不去。“如今，布莱顿市的榆树总数约17000棵。似乎当地有些因素能够帮助它们存活下来。来自海上的强风使顽固的榆树皮甲虫难以攻击这座沿海城市的榆树群。然而，情况仍然岌岌可危。“威胁近在咫尺，要是我们不小心，甲虫就会入侵。“伯恩说。[br][br]
[b] F. [/b] 榆树未来是否能够回归，很大程度上取决于树木对这种疾病是否具有抵抗力或耐受力。这意味着大规模重新引入将涉及(使用)现有的或新的杂交品种，这些品种源自有抵抗力的、通常是非本地的榆树物种。新一代的幼苗已经培育出来并进行了测试:通过在树皮上切一个小切口并注入少量病原体，来观察它们是否能抵抗这种真菌。“效果很快就会显现，“拉塞尔说。“四到六周后你再去看这些树，有抵抗力的树木没有任何症状，而易受感染的树则会落叶，甚至可能完全死亡。[br][br]
[b] G. [/b] 拉塞尔承认，所有这些(做法)会引发社会接受度的问题。“如果我们把榆树重新引入当地/景观中，且其中有一小部分是非本地的树--我们会担心吗?“对她来说，重新引入榆树，从环境角度出发，理由是很充分的。“它们会为野生动物提供栖息地，这是一件好事。“其他人则更为谨慎。"表面上看，这似乎是个好主意，“埃利奥特说。他认为问题在于，“你在用一种园艺类似物取代本地物种。实际上你是在克隆。“这还会有引入新疾病的风险。林地信托基金强调的是要为那些独立存活下来的榆树提供生长空间，而不是主张种植新的榆树。“有时候，你能做到最好的事情，就是(这些给大自然时间去恢复.…随着时间的推移，榆树)可能会产生抵抗力。“埃利奥特说。[br][br]
*园艺类似物:一种与现有物种基因相似的栽培物物种*[注释:“园艺类似物“就是人工培育的、与天然物种相似的“替代品”。
obj.html_content:
Mark Rowe investigates attempts to reintroduce elms to Britain




aAround 25 million elms, accounting for 90% of all elm trees in the UK, died during the 1960s and '70s of Dutch elm disease. In the aftermath, the elm, once so dominant in the British landscape, was largely forgotten. However, there's now hope the elm may be reintroduced to the countryside of central and southern England. Any reintroduction will start from a very low base. 'The impact of the disease is difficult to picture if you hadn't seen what was there before,' says Matt Elliot of the Woodland Trust. 'You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it's only then that you realise the impact [elms had] … They were significant, large trees… then they were gone.'



bThe disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the elms' vascular (water, nutrient and food transport) system, causing branches to wilt and die. A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the '70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. This time, the beetles carried a much more virulent strain that destroyed the vast majority of British elms.

cToday, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. 'We have millions of small elms in hedgerows but they get targeted by the beetle as soon as they reach a certain size,' says Karen Russell, co-author of the report 'Where we are with elm'. Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10-15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold. Yet mature specimens have been identified, in counties such as Cambridgeshire, that are hundreds of years old, and have mysteriously escaped the epidemic.

The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small. 'What are the reasons for their survival?' asks Russell. 'Avoidance, tolerance, resistance? We don't know where the balance lies between the three. I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck.'

dFor centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. Elm was also the preferred material for shields and early swords. In the 18th century, it was planted more widely and its wood was used for items such as storage crates and flooring. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.

eGiven how ingrained elm is in British culture, it's unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. 'I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,' he says. 'The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.' Today, the city of Brighton's elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city's elm population. However, the situation is precarious. 'The beetles can just march in if we're not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,' says Bourne.

fAny prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit on the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. 'The effects are very quick,' says Russell. 'You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.'

gAll of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. 'If we're putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native – are we bothered about that?' For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. 'They will host wildlife, which is a good thing. Others are more wary. 'On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,' says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, 'You're replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You're effectively cloning.' There's also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. 'Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover over time, you might get resistance,' says Elliot.

* horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species

obj.content:
[b][center]Uses of a popular tree[/center][/b][br][br]
For hundreds of years, the only tree that was more popular in Britain than elm was [blank]24[/blank]. Starting in the Bronze Age, many tools were made from elm and people also used it to make weapons. In the 18th century, it was grown to provide wood for boxes and [blank]25[/blank]. Due to its strength, elm was often used for mining equipment and the Cutty Sark's [blank]26[/blank] was also constructed from elm.
obj.html_content:
Uses of a popular tree



For hundreds of years, the only tree that was more popular in Britain than elm was . Starting in the Bronze Age, many tools were made from elm and people also used it to make weapons. In the 18th century, it was grown to provide wood for boxes and . Due to its strength, elm was often used for mining equipment and the Cutty Sark's was also constructed from elm.

obj.title: Return of the elm: reintroducing the beloved tree to Britain

'You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it's only then that you realise the impact [elms had] … They were significant, large trees… then they were gone.'[br/][br/]
[p:b]The disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the elms' vascular (water, nutrient and food transport) system, causing branches to wilt and die. A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the '70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. [p:c]Today, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. We don't know where the balance lies between the three. I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck.'[br][br]
[p:d]For centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.[br][br]
[p:e]Given how ingrained elm is in British culture, it's unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. The problem, he suggests, is that, 'You're replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You're effectively cloning.' There's also the risk of introducing new diseases. * horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species
obj.content_zh:
[br/][center]马克·罗（Mark Rowe）对英国重新引种榆树的计划进行了调研。[/center][br/][br/]
[b] A. [/b] 20世纪60年代至70年代，英国约2500万棵榆树(占英国所有榆树的90%)死于荷兰榆树病。此后，曾经在英国景观中占主导地位的榆树，很大程度上被遗忘了。然而，现在有希望将榆树重新引入英格兰中部和南部的乡村地区。所有重新引入的工作都将从一个非常低的起点开始。“如果你没有见过那里以前的景象，就很难想象这种疾病带来的影响，“林地信托基金的马特·埃利奥特说。“你得看看20世纪60年代的老照片，才会意识到榆树曾经的影响(有多大)它们(那时)是重要的巨大树木..接着就消失了。[br/][br/]
[b] B. [/b] 这种疾病是由一种真菌引起的，这种真菌会阻塞榆树的维管系统(水分、营养和食物运输系统)，导致树枝枯萎死亡。该病20世纪20年代发生的第一次疫情/传播逐渐平息，但在70年代，从加拿大运来的榆树引发了第二次传播。这些木材是原木形式运来的，用于造船，其完好的树皮为传播这种致命真菌的榆树皮甲虫提供了理想的生存环境。这一次，甲虫携带了一种毒性更强的菌株，摧毁了英国绝大多数的榆树。[br][br]
[b] C. [/b] 如今，英格兰南部的乡村仍有榆树，但大多只存在于田野间低矮的树篱中。“在树篱中有数百万棵小榆树，但它们一长到特定大小就会成为甲虫的目标，“《榆树现状报告》的合著者卡伦·拉塞尔说。“一旦榆树的树干直径达到10-15厘米左右就达到了甲虫产卵和真菌滋生的理想大小。然而，在剑桥郡等郡，人们发现了一些有数百年树龄的成熟榆树，它们不可思议地躲过了这场疫病。[br][br]
拉塞尔说，关键是要找出并研究那些存活下来的树木，弄清楚为什么在数百万棵榆树病死的情况下，它们还能存活下来。然而，由于这些存活到成熟的榆树相对较少，研究机会有限。“它们存活下来的原因是什么?“拉塞尔问道。“是它们避开疫病，还是对这种病有耐受力或者抵抗力?(但是)我们不知道这三者之间的平衡在哪里。我不认为这完全是运气。“[注释:"Where we arewith elm'是一份关于榆树现状的报告。[br][br]
[b] D. [/b] 几个世纪以来，在英国，榆树作为硬木树种在受欢迎程度方面仅次于橡树，而且在许多情况下，它是景观中最显眼的树。榆树不仅在欧洲森林中常见，还是桦树、白蜡树和榛树林的重要组成部分。榆树的使用可以追溯到青铜时代，当时它被广泛用于(制作)工具。榆树也是制盾和早期制剑的首选材料。18世纪，榆树被更广泛地种植，其木材被用于制作储物箱和地板等物品。它也适用于(制作)承受高强度撞击的物品，19世纪的帆船“卡蒂萨克号“的龙骨以及采矿设备就都是用榆树制作的。[br][br]

'You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it's only then that you realise the impact [elms had] … They were significant, large trees… then they were gone.'

A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the '70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. This time, the beetles carried a much more virulent strain that destroyed the vast majority of British elms.

cToday, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. 'We have millions of small elms in hedgerows but they get targeted by the beetle as soon as they reach a certain size,' says Karen Russell, co-author of the report 'Where we are with elm'. Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10-15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold. Yet mature specimens have been identified, in counties such as Cambridgeshire, that are hundreds of years old, and have mysteriously escaped the epidemic.

The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small. 'What are the reasons for their survival?' asks Russell. 'Avoidance, tolerance, resistance? We don't know where the balance lies between the three. I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck.'

dFor centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. Elm was also the preferred material for shields and early swords. In the 18th century, it was planted more widely and its wood was used for items such as storage crates and flooring. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.

eGiven how ingrained elm is in British culture, it's unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. 'I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,' he says. 'The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.' Today, the city of Brighton's elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city's elm population. However, the situation is precarious. 'The beetles can just march in if we're not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,' says Bourne.

fAny prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit on the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. 'The effects are very quick,' says Russell. 'You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.'

gAll of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. 'If we're putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native – are we bothered about that?' For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. 'They will host wildlife, which is a good thing. Others are more wary. 'On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,' says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, 'You're replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You're effectively cloning.' There's also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. 'Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover over time, you might get resistance,' says Elliot.

* horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species

sectionSheet.title: Return of the elm: reintroducing the beloved tree to Britain

officialExplain.explain:
译文：提及了由于仅有少量成年榆树幸存而造成的研究困难

在“段落匹配信息”这种类型的题目中，经常会有诸如reference / mention / account这类词汇出现，它们表达的意思都是“提到、说到”，属于对定位没有任何帮助的“废话词”，可以忽略不看。特别是reference一词，在其它语境中还可以有“参考”和“参考书目”的含义，但在这种匹配题中却并不表达这个意思，考生切勿出现误解，徒增理解难度

本题的解答难度并不高，因为位置靠前而段意也不难理解。其实Section C的第二自然段除了第一句话交代出“关键问题在于研究那些幸存成年树”这个背景外，剩下的全文都是在花式表达这么做很有难度，也就是对应题干中的research problems这个信息。与题干另一部分内容匹配度最高的是本段第二句，直接指出“研究机会并不多，因为存活下来的成年树就很少”。考生只需耐心读到此处，其实就可以得出本题答案的对应段号：C。

译文：关于重新在英国引入榆树这种做法之价值的不同看法细节

题干中的details这个词，诚然回到对应原文段中，势必要以具体详尽的更多内容来呈现说明；然而，却基本不太可能出现details这个词本词，因为几乎没有作者会在动笔直接描述细节前先来一句多此一举的“接下来我打算给出一些细节描述”。从这个角度来说，details这个词也基本算是本题型题干中常见的一个“废话词”，不应浪费考生的过多关注去寻找原词对应。

本题最大的难点可能在于位置靠后：考生要一直来到最后一段、且基本看完至少是大半段内容，才能更笃定地进行内容上的对应选择。本段的前四句给出的是Russell的看法，她认为虽然也有“使用非本土榆树品种”这个小问题，但总体来说重新引入还是有环保价值的；从第五句话开始，则出现了Elliot的不同看法，他指出这么做有新增疾病等风险，应当等着自然界自己恢复更好。这些表述，就是在用具体文字来给出不同观点的细节内容，本题的答案段号当然就应当是：G。

译文：提到了荷兰榆树病是如何被带入英国的

由于题干中的大写信息Dutch早在全文开篇第一句里就出现过了，所以本题显然不能仅仅依靠它来精准定位；但好在对应位置靠前：就在第二段第二句话，考生应当很快就能顺利阅读到这里、读出“第二场疫病是由从加拿大运输榆木木材所触发的”，进而锁定本题对应的段号为：B。

译文：描述了一些条件使得英国某地躲过了荷兰榆树病

从题干中可知，本题对应的段落必定需要提到一个英国的具体地点，且此处的榆树未受荷兰榆树病的很大影响。考生需要耐心逐段阅读，才能更稳妥地最终在本段第六和七题中看到对应信息：“本地条件对榆树生存做了贡献，具体来说是海上强风使得甲虫不易攻击当地榆树”，由此确定本题出处段落应为：E。

译文：提到了嫩榆树会在哪个阶段容易受到荷兰榆树病的侵袭

本题在文中的位置其实早于同样也出自这个section里的第14题，再次提醒了考生要注意：对于解答这类乱序题目，每看一部分文章内容，就需要耐心比对14-18中所有还未找到答案的题干，才能确保没有遗漏、只看一遍文章而准确定位解答每道题。看懂题干和对应原文这个任务本身倒是不困难：section C第一段里的第二句已经说到了“矮树篱中长了许多小榆树，但它们只要到达一定尺寸就会被甲虫锁定目标”；到了第三句，更是直接给出了具体数据：“一旦树身直径长到了10-15厘米，虫卵和真菌就来骚扰了”。看懂了这部分内容，也就能得出本题所对应的段号答案：C。

译文：如果一棵树感染了荷兰榆树病，损害很快就能肉眼可见。

本题的对应位置比较靠后，但只需考生笃定采用平行阅读法、逐段阅读对比，就能在终于来到倒数第二段的最后一句时看到相应内容：“（试验性给榆树苗注入少量病菌），四到六周后再来看，没染病的就会完好无恙，而染病植株就会大量落叶甚至完全死亡”，这正是对应了题干所说的“后果很快显现”，因此表达此观点的Russell就是正确答案项：B。

译文：也许等等看那些存活下来的成年榆树是否会持续繁荣才是更好的。

本题最大的难点也许在于定位：考生需要一直来到全文最后一句话，才能看到对应题干的表达。Elliot认为“最好的办法是给自然界时间让其自己恢复”，结合本段前文所说，也就是“不应人工引进非本地榆树物种，而是应当顺其自然等着那些存活下来的榆树自己发展出抵抗力”，考生应当就能锁定选项应为：A。

译文：一些成年榆树之所以能幸存下来总得有个原因。

与本题同样出现在这一段里的，还有前面的第14题，因此如果考生采用了平行阅读来解答所有题目，就可以一箭双雕，同时找到两个题型中的两道题目。第14题主要对应了本段的前两句话，Russell指出研究关键在于找到和研究那些存活下来的榆树，但它们数量不多因此机会也少。而本段接下来的几句话，基本都以直接引用Russell的发言来展开，反复陈述“虽然还搞不清楚具体原理，但总归事出有因、不可能完全是因为运气。”这些都对应了题干所说的“无论如何总会存在一个解释”，因此本题最合适的选项无疑就是：B。

译文：我们需要意识到携带荷兰榆树病的虫类离此并不遥远。

本题与第17题来自同一个段落，是平行阅读法可以帮助考生同时兼顾所有题型解答的又一个例证。本段的前文主要介绍布莱顿这个英国城市得天独厚，有助于当地榆树逃过致病甲虫的侵扰；但从倒数第二句开始话锋一转，也提醒人们情况还是不容乐观，因为这些虫子随时可能长驱直入卷土重来，这正是对应了题干的表述，因此答案项应为：C。

译文：当你看到榆树曾经多么举足轻重的证据后，你就理解了荷兰榆树病所造成的影响。

本题是这个题型中从定位角度来说最友好的一道：考生在第一自然段的后半部分中，就能看到Elliot在充满感情地表达对英国榆树大量死于荷兰榆树病的痛惜。基本上，引言中的这几句话就是在用更详尽的方式花式表述了题干内容：你要是没见过榆树林先前的盛况，就很难想象出这种病造成的影响。你得看看六七十年代那些老照片，只有在那时才能理解榆树的地位。它们曾是如此重要而雄伟的树木，后来却消失了。”考生只需大致看懂这些表述，就不难为本题匹配到最合适的选项：A。

译文：在几百年的时间里，唯一在英国比榆树更受欢迎的树种是____。

题干中的for hundreds of years在原文中仅仅只是潦草地替换成了for centuries这种简单变体，“受欢迎程度领先于榆树”的表达回到原文中也只是调换了顺序、变成了“榆树仅仅只是紧跟橡树之后屈居第二”。虽然要找原文原词对应并不容易，但这两个英文表述本身的理解难度都并不高，考生只需看懂句意，就能顺利得出答案应为：oak。

译文：在18世纪种植它是为了给箱子和____提供木材。

本题可由数字信息“18世纪”引导着直接对应到这一段的第五句话里。可能解题的唯一困难在于对应题干boxes的原文用词crates对一些考生来说会稍显陌生，但crates之前的storage又非常友好地暗示了这个用作“储藏”的生词就是在替换“箱子、盒子”这类物件，帮助考生利用排除法锁定与之并列的另外一个词汇作为本题答案：flooring。

译文：由于其硬度，榆木经常被用以制作采矿设备，Cutty Sark的____也是用榆木制作的。

本题定位紧跟上一题，且包含了一模一样的题干信息mining equipment，考生只需看懂原文中的“keel of ... Cutty Sack”这个A of B的结构就是改头换面替代了题干中的B’s ... 这个结构，那么即使并不认识“龙骨”这个单词，也能顺利锁定本题空格中的答案应为：keel。
